title: Get your I-20 from your university
description: Your I-20 is the document that authorizes your F-1 student status. It is issued by your university's international student office (DSO). Everything else — your visa, your SSN, your OPT — depends on this document.
common_mistakes: Not updating your I-20 when you change your program or extend your studies
Thinking your visa expiring means your F-1 status expired — they are different
Not carrying a physical copy — always have it on you

title: Pay the SEVIS I-901 fee
description: A one-time $350 fee required before your visa interview. You pay this at fmjfee.com using your SEVIS ID from your I-20. Keep the payment confirmation — you'll need it at the visa interview.
common_mistakes: Paying after scheduling your visa interview — pay before
Losing the payment receipt — save a PDF copy

title: Get your F-1 visa stamp
description: Schedule a visa interview at a US consulate in your home country. Bring your I-20, SEVIS fee receipt, financial proof, and admission letter. The visa stamp is what lets you enter the US — it's not the same as your F-1 status.
common_mistakes: Not scheduling early enough — interviews book up months in advance
Wearing the wrong clothes for the visa photo

title: Arrive in the US and get your I-94
description: When you arrive, a CBP officer will admit you. Your I-94 is automatically generated — it records your admission date and status. Check it at i94.cbp.dhs.gov within a few days of arrival. It should say 'D/S' (Duration of Status) not a specific date.
common_mistakes: Not checking your I-94 after arrival — errors happen and must be corrected
Arriving more than 30 days before your program start date — not allowed on F-1

title: Check in with your international student office
description: Within your first week, report your arrival to your DSO (Designated School Official). They will activate your SEVIS record. You cannot legally study or work until this is done.
common_mistakes: Skipping this step — your SEVIS won't be activated and you'll be out of status
Waiting too long — do this in the first week

title: Get a local US address
description: You need a physical US address to open a bank account, receive mail, and register for classes. Options: university housing (easiest), short-term rental, or staying with a friend temporarily.
common_mistakes: Using a PO Box — banks don't accept these as a primary address
Using your university's main address — use your specific dorm or apartment address

title: Get a U.S. mobile number
description: A U.S. phone number makes it much easier to receive bank OTPs, landlord calls, campus alerts, delivery updates, and job follow-ups. Set one up soon after arrival through a major carrier or a prepaid provider.
common_mistakes: Choosing an expensive long-term plan before checking student discounts or prepaid options
Using only your home-country number for bank and delivery verification
Forgetting to confirm network coverage around campus and housing

title: Apply for an SSN (on-campus job route)
description: As an F-1 student, you can get an SSN if you have an on-campus job offer. Get a job offer letter from your department, then get a letter from your DSO, then visit your local Social Security Administration office.
common_mistakes: Going to SSA before you have all three letters
Going to SSA before your SEVIS is activated — wait at least 3 days after check-in

title: Apply for an ITIN (if you can't get an SSN yet)
description: An Individual Taxpayer Identification Number lets you open a bank account and file taxes without an SSN. Apply with IRS Form W-7. You can do this without a job offer. Takes 6-11 weeks to process.
common_mistakes: Sending your original passport — send certified copies only
Not attaching a tax return if required
Thinking you need an SSN to open a bank account — ITIN works

title: Open a US bank account
description: Most banks accept an ITIN or SSN plus your I-20 and passport. Recommended: Chase, Bank of America, or a credit union. Call ahead to confirm they accept international students — some branches don't know their own policy.
common_mistakes: Going without an appointment — book online first
Not bringing your I-20 — banks often ask even if they don't advertise it
Choosing a bank with no branches near you — pick one you can walk to

title: Get a secured credit card to build credit
description: A secured card requires a deposit (usually $200-500) that becomes your credit limit. Use it for small purchases and pay it off every month. After 6-12 months you'll have a credit score. Recommended: Discover it Secured, Capital One Secured.
common_mistakes: Not paying the full balance every month — interest rates are 25%+
Applying for too many cards at once — hurts your credit score
Closing the card after getting a regular card — keep it open for credit history length

title: Build 6+ months of credit history
description: Use your secured card for small regular purchases (groceries, gas). Pay the full balance before the due date every single month. After 6 months you'll have a credit score. After 12 months you can apply for a regular credit card and rent without a guarantor.
common_mistakes: Missing a payment — even one late payment drops your score significantly
Using more than 30% of your credit limit — keep usage low

title: Apply for CPT (Curricular Practical Training)
description: CPT lets you work off-campus in a job directly related to your major while still a student. You must enroll in an internship course first. Apply through your international student office at least 7 business days before your start date.
common_mistakes: Starting work before CPT is authorized — this is a serious status violation
Applying with less than 7 business days before start date
Not enrolling in the required internship course

title: Apply for Post-OPT
description: Optional Practical Training lets you work in your field after graduation for 12 months. Apply up to 90 days before your graduation date. USCIS takes 3-5 months to process — apply early. You cannot start work until your EAD card arrives.
documents_needed: I-20 with OPT recommendation from DSO
common_mistakes: Applying too late — USCIS takes months and you can't work until EAD arrives
Starting work before EAD card arrives — automatic status violation
Not paying the $300 OPT Case Management Payment to your university first

title: Apply for STEM OPT Extension
description: If your degree is in a STEM field, you can extend your OPT by 24 months. You must apply before your current OPT expires. Your employer must be enrolled in E-Verify. CRITICAL: Apply within 60 days of your new I-20 issue date or you are automatically denied.
common_mistakes: Missing the 60-day window from I-20 issue date — automatic denial, no exceptions
Employer not completing their section of I-983 before you apply
Employer not enrolled in E-Verify — required, cannot be waived
Not filing Form I-983 annual self-evaluation on time
